skill.name: golang-enterprise-patterns
skill.description: Guides enterprise Go application design with clean architecture, hexagonal architecture, domain-driven design, dependency injection, configuration, and error handling patterns.
skill.summary: Apply enterprise Go architecture patterns for maintainable production services.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, cryptography, reconnaissance, and environment-access patterns, but review found a documentation-only Markdown skill. The command detections are fenced Go and text examples, the weak-crypto and reconnaissance detections are keyword false positives, and DATABASE_URL appears only in a configuration example. No prompt injection, malicious execution path, credential exfiltration, or network callback was found.

low_findings.title: Documentation Examples Trigger Static Command Detections
low_findings.description: The external command findings are false positives. The cited locations are Markdown fences and Go examples for project layout, repository interfaces, dependency injection, SQL execution, and configuration. They are not executable skill scripts and do not invoke a shell.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only scanned file is SKILL.md, and the cited ranges are fenced text or Go examples. No runnable script, shell command, or command construction path is present.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Mention Is Configuration Guidance
low_findings.description: The DATABASE_URL finding is a documentation example showing a Go configuration loader. It may reference a secret-bearing environment variable, but the skill itself does not read or transmit environment values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is inside a fenced Go example and assigns a database URL to an application config object. I found no evidence of runtime environment access by the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Findings Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections map to architecture and DDD wording, not cryptographic algorithms. The reconnaissance detections map to ordinary domain methods such as FindByID and configuration examples, not system discovery commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found no MD5, SHA-1, crypto API, host enumeration, process listing, or filesystem discovery behavior. The lines are architectural prose or ordinary Go domain examples.

content.value_statement: Large Go services can become hard to maintain when dependencies and business rules are mixed. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured architecture patterns for clean, testable Go applications.
actual_capabilities: Explains clean architecture layer boundaries for Go applications.
Shows ports and adapters patterns for primary and secondary interfaces.
Provides domain-driven design examples for aggregates, value objects, and events.
Demonstrates manual dependency injection and Wire-style initialization patterns.
Covers configuration loading and custom error handling for production services.
Suggests package structure for API, worker, domain, application, and infrastructure code.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a runnable application generator.
Examples are illustrative and may need imports, tests, and project-specific details.
It does not choose databases, queues, or frameworks for a specific production environment.
It does not replace security, performance, or reliability reviews of generated code.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Backend Service
use_cases.description: Define package boundaries, ports, adapters, and dependency flow before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Refactor a Growing Go Codebase
use_cases.description: Separate domain logic from infrastructure and handlers without changing product behavior.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Architecture
use_cases.description: Create shared patterns for repositories, services, errors, configuration, and dependency injection.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Clean Architecture Outline
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new service
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the golang-enterprise-patterns skill to outline a clean architecture package structure for a Go service that manages users and orders.

prompt_templates.title: Define Ports and Adapters
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the golang-enterprise-patterns skill to design primary and secondary ports for a Go billing service with HTTP APIs, PostgreSQL storage, and email notifications.

prompt_templates.title: Model a Domain Aggregate
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the golang-enterprise-patterns skill to model an order aggregate with value objects, domain events, and validation rules for a marketplace checkout flow.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Enterprise Go Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the golang-enterprise-patterns skill to review this Go project structure for dependency direction, domain purity, interface boundaries, and production readiness.

output_examples.input: Design the structure for a new Go API service.
output: A package layout that separates command entry points, domain models, application services, infrastructure adapters, and interface handlers.
A short explanation of which layer owns each responsibility and which dependencies are allowed.

output_examples.input: Help refactor repository and service dependencies.
output: A recommended interface boundary where domain or application code owns contracts and infrastructure provides implementations.
A constructor-based dependency plan that keeps concrete databases and transports out of business logic.

output_examples.input: Review domain-driven design for an order workflow.
output: A proposed aggregate root, value object boundaries, domain events, and invariant checks.
Notes about keeping persistence and framework concerns outside the domain model.

best_practices: Keep domain rules independent from frameworks, databases, and transport details.
Use small interfaces that describe the behavior each use case actually needs.
Pass dependencies explicitly through constructors so tests can replace infrastructure.
anti_patterns: Do not let HTTP handlers call database code directly for complex business workflows.
Do not place framework annotations or persistence details inside core domain types.
Do not create broad repository interfaces that expose methods no use case needs.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a complete Go service?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture guidance and examples that you adapt to your service.

faq.question: Can I use it with existing Go projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide refactoring toward clearer layers, interfaces, and dependency direction.

faq.question: Does it require Google Wire?
faq.answer: No. It includes Wire-style examples, but it also recommends manual dependency injection for simplicity.

faq.question: Is it only for domain-driven design?
faq.answer: No. It also covers clean architecture, hexagonal architecture, configuration, and error handling.

faq.question: Will the examples compile as shown?
faq.answer: Not always. They are abbreviated examples and may need imports, supporting types, and tests.

faq.question: Does it access my database or environment variables?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guidance skill and only includes configuration examples.
